subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith

items.original_candidate: Coffeyville (MS, near Yazoo) or "Hot Coffee" (Covington County MS)
items.evidence: The context is Raylawni Branch describing her grandmother fleeing pregnancy from 'a little area out near Prentiss, Mississippi, Coffinchett' and running off to Hattiesburg. External verification of place-names in Jefferson Davis County (county seat: Prentiss) and adjacent Covington County yielded the following documented communities near Prentiss: Bassfield, Bethel, Carson, Clem, Greens Creek, Gwinville, Hathorn, Mount Carmel, Oak Vale, Williamsburg, and Old Hebron (Jefferson Davis); Hot Coffee, Mount Olive (Covington). NONE phonetically match 'Coffinchett.' Coffeyville/Coffeville MS are in Yalobusha County (far north MS) — geographically implausible for a 'little area out near Prentiss.' Hot Coffee is ~17 mi from Prentiss but is a single-word name. The original candidate Pass 1/Pass 2 hypotheses (Coffeyville, Hot Coffee, Coffeville) cannot be confidently confirmed. Retain Whisper rendering flagged as unverified to avoid fabricating a canonical place-name that does not exist in archival records. Smithsonian-grade discipline: literal Whisper with [unverified] flag is preferable to phonetic guess.

Branch identifies him as 'the Black principal ... over in Rowan,' referring to Rowan High School in Hattiesburg. Multiple independent archival sources (Historic Eureka School profile; Burger Middle School official history; HubCitySPOKES; angelfire/EUROHA historical sketch of Eureka-Royal Street-Rowan) confirm Nathaniel R. Burger ('N.R. Burger') served as principal of Eureka High School from 1940, continuing through its renaming/transition to Royal Street and then Rowan High School, until his retirement in 1974. This squarely covers the late-1950s period (Clyde Kennard's USM attempts) the speaker is recounting. Burger Middle School in Hattiesburg is named for him. The 'Berger' spelling is the Whisper artifact for the canonical surname Burger.

items.new_candidate: Dr. Mason / Dr. (Charles E. or Charles W.) Smith [Hattiesburg local Black-physician network — specific identities of 'Dr. Mason' unverified]
items.evidence: The transcript context is Jeanette Smith describing her husband and his physician colleagues: 'They had an organization among themselves' (Hattiesburg Black-doctor network) — distinct from but adjacent to the Medical Committee for Human Rights (MCHR). Earlier in the same answer, the interviewer asks about MCHR; Jeanette says 'they were friends and he worked with him, Dr. Mason, Dr. Smith, and I.' The 'Dr. Smith' reference is almost certainly to her own husband, Dr. Charles E. Smith (Meharry MD 1957, began Hattiesburg practice 1958, NAACP Forrest County president 1967-1972 — Hattiesburg Eureka School historical record) and/or Dr. Charles W. Smith (Black physician with office above Smith Drug Co. on Mobile Street, one of the 15 NAACP voting-rights plaintiffs of 1950). 'Dr. Mason' cannot be confidently identified as Dr. James Mason or Dr. Gilbert R. Mason (Biloxi) without further archival evidence — Dr. Gilbert R. Mason was Biloxi-based (beach wade-ins), geographically removed from Hattiesburg's local physician network the speaker is describing. The original Pass 1 hypothesis of 'Dr. Aaron Shirley' is rejected — Shirley was Jackson-based and is not documented as a Hattiesburg collaborator. Retain 'Dr. Mason / Dr. Smith' as spoken; flag Mason's specific canonical identity as unverified.

items.original_candidate: Pearson v. Murray (1936) / Missouri ex rel. Gaines v. Canada (1938) — out-of-state tuition equalization
items.new_candidate: [paying tuition] (the Mississippi segregation-scholarship out-of-state tuition program established 1948)
items.evidence: The interviewer's clarifying remark immediately following the Whisper-garbled phrase makes the referent unambiguous: 'this is before Brown, and this is when they were trying to avoid ... separate but equal.' The speaker (Raylawni Branch, a 1950s Hattiesburg high school student) is recounting how teacher Mrs. Marjorie James told her about a Mississippi statute permitting Black students to attend graduate/professional school out-of-state at state expense — the canonical Southern 'segregation scholarship' or out-of-state tuition equalization gambit. External verification: Bunk History and multiple legal-history sources confirm Mississippi established its out-of-state tuition program in 1948 to evade in-state graduate school integration after Missouri ex rel. Gaines v. Canada (1938) required equal in-state provision. 'Peyton DeVille' is NOT a case name — Pearson v. Murray (1936, Maryland) and Gaines (1938, Missouri) are real cases but neither is what the speaker uttered. The phonetically plausible reading of 'Peyton DeVille' is 'paying tuition' (or 'paying that tuition' / 'paid out-of-state tuition'). The Whisper output is a phonetic mis-transcription of the everyday phrase 'paying tuition,' which fits both the syllable count and the contextual meaning (the speaker is describing how Mississippi 'paid' for Black students to study elsewhere). Reject the Pass 1 case-name hypotheses; substitute the plain-English reading.

items.original_candidate: Coffee (?) — uncertain Jefferson Davis County MS community
items.evidence: Duplicate of row 100.11 — same Whisper rendering, same context (grandmother's flight 'from a little area out near Prentiss'). Exhaustive search of Jefferson Davis County and adjacent Covington County place-name registers (MS GenWeb, Wikipedia category listings, MSGHN local communities pages) returned no community named or phonetically close to 'Coffinchett.' Hot Coffee (Covington County) is the closest documented coffee-derived place-name but is a different spelling and word-shape. Without archival corroboration, retain Whisper rendering flagged unverified. Cross-references 100.11.

items.original_candidate: (uncertain — possibly *Pearson v. Murray* (1936) or *Missouri ex rel. Gaines v. Canada* (1938))
items.evidence: Cross-references 100.63. The interviewer immediately glosses the speaker's statement as 'before Brown ... trying to avoid ... separate but equal,' which exactly matches the Mississippi 1948 out-of-state tuition statute (segregation scholarship), the canonical pre-Brown gambit Southern states used to evade in-state graduate-school integration after Gaines v. Canada (1938). 'Peyton DeVille' is a Whisper mis-transcription of the everyday phrase 'paying tuition' — phonetically consistent with the syllable structure (PAY-ton-de-VILLE ≈ PAY-ing tu-i-TION), and semantically consistent with the speaker's point (Mississippi 'paid' tuition for Black students to attend out-of-state graduate schools). Neither Pearson v. Murray nor Missouri ex rel. Gaines is a case the speaker would have named informally with that phonetic shape. Reject the case-name candidates; substitute the plain-English reading.

items.original_candidate: Dr. James Mason / Dr. Smith / (likely Dr. Aaron Shirley + Dr. James Anderson MCHR-MS chapter members)
items.new_candidate: Dr. Mason / Dr. (Charles E. or Charles W.) Smith [Hattiesburg Black-physician network; Mason's specific identity unverified]
items.evidence: Cross-references 100.53. The Pass 2 hypothesis (Dr. Aaron Shirley + Dr. James Anderson) is rejected — those are Jackson-based MCHR-MS chapter doctors, not Hattiesburg colleagues of Jeanette Smith's husband. The Hattiesburg-local context (a Black-doctor organization 'among themselves,' distinct from MCHR which Marian Wright Edelman is mentioned with in the same exchange) points to Dr. Charles E. Smith (Meharry 1957, Hattiesburg practice 1958, NAACP Forrest County president 1967-1972) and/or Dr. Charles W. Smith (medical office on Mobile Street above Smith Drug Co., one of the 15 NAACP voting-rights plaintiffs of 1950). 'Dr. Mason' cannot be confidently identified — Dr. Gilbert R. Mason (Biloxi wade-ins, MCHR) is geographically distant from the Hattiesburg local-network the speaker describes; Dr. James Mason is not documented as a Hattiesburg figure in any source I reviewed. Retain literal Whisper rendering for Mason; flag as unverified.

items.original_candidate: (N.R. Burger) — likely Pass 1 candidate / different from Hattiesburg N.R. Burger
items.new_candidate: Mr. (N.R.) Burger [Nathaniel R. Burger, principal of Rowan High School]
items.evidence: Cross-references 100.42. Same context (Branch identifying the Rowan High School Black principal). The Pass 2 note's wavering about 'different from Hattiesburg N.R. Burger' is itself incorrect — Branch's reference IS to the Hattiesburg N.R. Burger. Multiple sources confirm Nathaniel R. Burger was principal of Eureka/Royal Street/Rowan High School from 1940 to 1974, was a prominent Hattiesburg community leader, and has Burger Middle School named for him today. Branch's complaint that 'they wanted a Black junior college put here in Hattiesburg [instead of integration]' aligns with Burger's documented gradualist negotiating posture with USM president William D. McCain. Confirm the canonical reading.

items.new_candidate: Marion [first-name-only; Chinese-American co-founder of Jill Wakeman's San Francisco food kitchen — surname unverified]
items.evidence: Branch describes Jill Wakeman (Hattiesburg civil-rights volunteer who stayed with a local family on Tipton/Anystrunzel St.) as having later 'opened the biggest soup kitchen, still running in San Francisco' with a Chinese-American co-founder named Marion. External verification confirms Jill Wakeman as a documented long-time SF Bay Area civil-rights activist (ABC7 News profile, Mississippi summer 1964 volunteer). However, no public source I found names the Chinese-American co-founder Marion (or Marian) of any specific San Francisco soup kitchen tied to Wakeman; the ABC7 article mentions only her later volunteer work at Call Primrose in Burlingame, not a co-founded SF soup kitchen. The Marion/Marian spelling distinction cannot be archivally adjudicated. Retain 'Marion' as spoken (Branch repeats 'Marion. Marion. Marion.' three times in the SRT, confirming her own pronunciation), but flag the canonical identity as unverified — Branch herself notes 'I [forget] her [last name]' in the surrounding passage.
